g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on vfork, a special system call for efficient process creation in Unix.
vfork creates a new process without copying the parent's memory space. The child shares the parent's address space directly.
g.text: A system call that creates a new process
WITHOUT copying the parent's address space.
Child shares parent's memory until calling exec() or exiting

g.data-narration: vfork was created for performance. When a child immediately calls exec to load a new program, copying memory is wasteful.
g.text: In early Unix, memory was expensive. Full fork() was too slow
when child immediately replaces itself with exec().
vfork = fork + exec optimization - zero memory copy!

g.data-narration: The vfork function has the same prototype as fork, returning the child's PID to parent, zero to child, or -1 on error.
g.text: Returns 0 to child | Child's PID to parent | -1 on error

g.data-narration: Compare fork and vfork: fork creates a copy (or uses copy-on-write), while vfork shares memory directly with parent.
g.text: fork(): Both processes continue independently | vfork(): Parent blocked until child exec/exit

g.data-narration: With vfork, child and parent share code, data, heap, and stack. Any modification by child immediately affects parent.
g.text: ⚠️ Child modifications to variables affect parent immediately! Must be careful!

g.data-narration: The execution sequence: parent calls vfork, kernel creates child without copying memory, child runs in parent's space, parent blocks until child exits or execs.
g.text: 5. Child MUST call exec() or _exit() → 6. Parent resumes execution

g.data-narration: Critical requirement: child must call exec to load a new program or exit with _exit. Returning without these corrupts parent's stack!
g.text: Child MUST do ONE of these:
call exec() to load new program OR call _exit() to terminate
⚠️ Returning corrupts parent's stack → Undefined Behavior!

g.data-narration: Always use _exit() not exit() in child after vfork. Using exit flushes parent's buffers, causing duplicate output.
g.text: Flushes parent's I/O buffers → Duplicate output!
Terminates child immediately, no buffer flush
After vfork in child: Always use _exit() for termination, never exit()!

g.data-narration: Here's a complete example showing memory sharing: child modifies data, parent sees the changes after child exits.
g.text: Note: Parent sees 20 because child modified the SAME variable in shared memory

g.data-narration: Even with copy-on-write, vfork remains useful in embedded systems and scenarios requiring guaranteed zero-copy behavior.
g.text: Modern Context: Is vfork Still Relevant?
Legacy compatibility + specialized use cases keep vfork() relevant today

g.data-narration: Let's summarize: vfork shares memory, child must exec or exit, use _exit not exit, parent blocks until child completes.

g.text: vfork() does NOT copy parent's address space - child shares memory
Child MUST call exec() or _exit() - returning without these causes undefined behavior
Use _exit() NOT exit() in child after vfork to avoid buffer corruption
Parent blocks until child calls exec() or terminates

g.data-narration: For exams: remember return values, the critical rules, and why vfork was created. Practice with code examples!
g.text: • Return: 0 to child, child's PID to parent, -1 on error
• fork() vs vfork(): Memory copy vs sharing
• NEVER return from calling function in child without exec()/_exit()
• vfork() parent is BLOCKED until child completes
⚠️ Most common mistake: Using exit() instead of _exit() in child
